name: Index Fossil Codebase Stratigraphy
executive_summary:
Index fossils are taxa with three defining properties: wide geographic distribution, short stratigraphic range (narrow temporal window of existence), and high preservation probability. These properties make them ideal dateable markers: finding an index fossil in a rock layer constrains that layer's age to a tight window regardless of other information. The method works without any absolute dating — relative temporal position is recoverable from pattern alone.

Legacy codebases accumulate identifiable strata of dependency vintages, syntactic idioms, API generations, and error-handling conventions that behave exactly like biostratigraphic assemblages. Specific constructs exist for narrow historical windows: jQuery 1.x chained DOM manipulation patterns, Python 2 print statements, Java EE deployment descriptor formats, callback-pyramid async patterns before Promise standardization, and dozens of analogous markers. These 'index idioms' have wide occurrence across codebases of their era, short historical ranges, and high detectability. Finding them in a codebase layer constrains that layer's composition date to a tight window.

This framework proposes formalizing codebase stratigraphy as a systematic discipline: cataloguing index idioms with their known date ranges and occurrence probabilities, developing automated scanners that identify stratigraphic assemblages in code, and producing technical debt maps that identify layer boundaries without access to version control history. This is practically significant because many inherited codebases have lost, corrupted, or falsified version control histories, and even intact histories do not reveal the temporal distribution of technical decisions within a large codebase.

mapped_components.unit: A syntactic construct, dependency declaration, API call pattern, or error-handling idiom — the 'index idiom' analogous to an index fossil taxon
mapped_components.variation: Variation in idiom adoption rates across codebases, developer backgrounds, and organizational contexts
mapped_components.selection: Adoption pressure from framework migrations, deprecation notices, and security advisories that cause idioms to be replaced — analogous to extinction events that terminate a taxon's range
mapped_components.fitness: Detection probability: the likelihood that a given idiom, if present in code written during its window, will be detectable by static analysis — the analog of preservation probability
mapped_components.boundary: A stratigraphic layer boundary in a codebase: a functional module, subsystem, or file cluster with a coherent index idiom assemblage indicating a constrained composition window

falsifiable_predictions: Automated static analysis of open-source repositories with known commit histories will show that index idiom assemblages correctly predict the median file creation year within a 3-year window for at least 75% of file clusters, benchmarked against actual commit timestamps from git history.

Codebases with sharp architectural boundaries (e.g., service-oriented refactors, major framework migrations) will show discontinuous idiom assemblage shifts at those boundaries, while codebases with organic growth will show gradual assemblage transitions — falsifiable by comparing architectural documentation against stratigraphic maps.

The 'stratigraphic age' of a codebase module, as estimated by index idiom assemblage, will predict its defect density better than raw lines-of-code or cyclomatic complexity, because older strata carry accumulated technical debt not captured by size metrics.

For any pair of programming languages with overlapping historical windows, the index idiom method will produce age estimates with tighter confidence intervals for the language with richer syntactic evolution history (e.g., JavaScript vs. COBOL), because more idiom generations provide more stratigraphic resolution.

Codebase modules identified as 'old stratum' by index idiom assemblage but lacking old-stratum indicators in version control metadata will show disproportionate rates of security vulnerabilities from known CVE classes associated with deprecated patterns, providing an independent validation signal.

supporting_evidence.finding: Software aging research has demonstrated that files with higher 'code age' — measured by time since last modification — have systematically higher defect rates, and that age distribution within a codebase is highly non-uniform, creating effective strata of technical debt concentration.
supporting_evidence.relevance: This establishes that temporal layering exists and is consequential within codebases, validating the core stratigraphic premise that different code regions reflect different compositional periods with different quality profiles.
supporting_evidence.source: Graves, T.L., Karr, A.F., Marron, J.S., and Siy, H. (2000). Predicting fault incidence using software change history. IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering, 26(7), 653–661.

supporting_evidence.finding: Empirical studies of JavaScript ecosystem evolution have documented that specific API patterns (e.g., XMLHttpRequest usage, CommonJS module syntax, callback-first async patterns) cluster tightly in historical windows corresponding to pre-Promise, pre-ES6, and pre-async/await periods respectively, with sharp adoption curves.
supporting_evidence.relevance: This directly empirically validates the 'index idiom' concept: these patterns have narrow historical ranges and high occurrence probability within their windows, meeting the index fossil criteria.
supporting_evidence.source: Hilton, M., Tunnell, T., Huang, K., Dig, D., and Marinov, D. (2016). Usage, costs, and benefits of continuous integration in open-source projects. Proceedings of the 31st IEEE/ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE), 426–437.

supporting_evidence.finding: Biostratigraphic correlation methods using assemblage zones rather than single index taxa are more robust to regional variation in taxon occurrence — the same principle applies to multi-idiom assemblage dating of codebases, where no single idiom is universal but assemblages are diagnostic.
supporting_evidence.relevance: This supports the key methodological choice of using assemblage-level analysis rather than single-idiom detection, directly importing the stratigraphic practice of biozone correlation into the codebase context.
supporting_evidence.source: Gradstein, F.M., Ogg, J.G., Schmitz, M., and Ogg, G. (eds.) (2012). The Geologic Time Scale 2012. Elsevier, Amsterdam. (Chapter 2: 'Biostratigraphy and the Geological Time Scale'.)

experimental_designs.name: Open-Source Repository Blind Dating Benchmark
experimental_designs.description: Select 100 open-source repositories with intact git histories spanning at least 10 years. Strip all metadata (commit timestamps, version tags, changelogs) from analysis inputs. Apply index idiom assemblage analysis using a pre-compiled catalog of 50+ dated idioms per language. Produce stratigraphic age estimates for each file cluster. Compare estimates against actual commit timestamp distributions recovered from the stripped histories. Report mean absolute error, 90th percentile error, and correlation between estimated and actual age distributions.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Index idiom assemblage analysis will achieve a mean absolute error of 3 years or less for file cluster age estimation on codebases with substantial syntactic evolution history (Python, JavaScript, Java), validating the stratigraphic dating method.
experimental_designs.feasibility: High — large open-source repositories with long histories are freely available (GitHub, GitLab), idiom catalogs can be compiled from published deprecation timelines, and static analysis tools for pattern detection are mature and language-specific implementations exist.

experimental_designs.name: Technical Debt Stratigraphic Mapping vs. Manual Audit Comparison
experimental_designs.description: Commission concurrent technical debt audits of 5 large enterprise codebases (100k+ LOC) using (a) conventional manual expert review, (b) automated index idiom stratigraphic mapping, and (c) a combined method. Measure defect discovery rate, audit time, and cost for each approach. Additionally, track which codebase modules flagged as 'old stratum' by the stratigraphic method subsequently produce security incidents or production failures over a 12-month follow-up period.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Stratigraphic mapping will identify high-risk code strata with precision comparable to expert audit at 20% of the cost, and modules flagged as old stratum will show a statistically significant higher rate of subsequent security incidents than non-flagged modules.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Medium — requires enterprise partnerships and longitudinal tracking, but the audit comparison component can be run independently as a validity check, and several firms already conduct technical debt audits that could host the comparison arm.

limitations: Index idiom date ranges vary significantly across organizational contexts: a company with conservative upgrade policies may use Python 2 idioms well into periods when Python 3 idioms are standard elsewhere, widening the confidence interval and reducing dating precision.

Deliberate code modernization (automated linting, mass refactoring tools) can strip index idioms from old strata without changing the underlying logic, producing misleadingly 'young' assemblage signals in genuinely old code — the stratigraphic equivalent of reworked sediment.

The index idiom catalog requires continuous maintenance as language ecosystems evolve; idioms that are currently 'active' do not have a closed stratigraphic range and cannot date recent code, creating a 'Recent' period analogous to the problem of dating Holocene sediments by biostratigraphy.

Multi-language codebases with components in languages at different stages of syntactic evolution will produce inconsistent stratigraphic resolution across modules, complicating whole-codebase age maps.

The method is language-specific and requires a separate idiom catalog per language family; for less common languages with smaller historical communities, the idiom catalog will be sparse and dating precision will be low.
potential_applications: Automated technical debt prioritization: generating stratigraphic maps of large codebases to identify old-stratum modules for refactoring queues without expensive manual audits.
Acquisition due diligence for software companies: rapidly estimating the compositional age distribution of an acquired codebase to assess hidden technical debt before deal closure.
Security vulnerability triage: identifying modules with old-stratum assemblages as high-priority targets for security review, since known vulnerability classes cluster by idiom generation.

Compliance archaeology: recovering the approximate implementation timeline of features in codebases where version control history has been lost, deleted, or falsified — relevant for regulatory audits of financial and medical software.

Developer onboarding tooling: generating stratigraphic maps that help new team members understand which codebase regions are 'living' versus 'fossil' code, improving navigation and reducing accidental modification of deprecated strata.

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Dead code detection identifies unreachable code but not its age; index fossil stratigraphy adds temporal depth to the spatial map that dead code analysis produces.
Stratigraphic age estimates provide the time variable needed to compute compound technical debt accumulation; old-stratum identification is a prerequisite for debt quantification models.

The Harris Matrix formalizes stratigraphic superposition relationships for archaeological contexts; an analogous matrix could formalize dependency and inheritance relationships between codebase strata, making the stratigraphy navigable.

connections_to_other_frameworks.framework: Signor-Lipps Bias Correction for Historical Record Gaps
connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Both frameworks apply paleontological occurrence methods to non-geological domains; the Signor-Lipps framework handles last-occurrence artifacts while stratigraphy handles layer-dating, and the two methods are complementary for reconstructing the history of any incompletely documented system.
